1.synthesis: 'When men began to multiply on the face of the land and daughters were born to them, the sons of God saw that the daughters of man were attractive. And they took as their wives any they chose. Then the LORD said, "My Spirit shall not abide in man forever, for he is flesh: his days shall be 120 years." The Nephilim were on the earth in those days … and also afterward, when the sons of God came in to the daughters of man and they bore children to them. These were the mighty men who were of old, the men of renown' (vv.1–4). The escalation to universal wickedness that required the flood does not arrive without narrative preparation: the intermarriage passage of vv.1–4, however difficult in interpretation, signals the breach of a boundary — between the line of Seth and the line of Cain, or between heavenly and earthly beings — that accelerated the moral collapse of vv.5–7. 'The LORD saw that the wickedness of man was great in the earth, and that every intention of the thoughts of his heart was only evil continually' (v.5) — a judgment of total pervasion: every, only, continually. 'And the LORD was sorry that he had made man on the earth, and it grieved him to his heart' (v.6). The divine grief is real and is the prelude to the most severe judgment in the pre-Mosaic narrative. Yet the section ends not with judgment but with a pivot: 'But Noah found favor in the eyes of the LORD' (v.8).
1.historical_context: The 'sons of God' (bĕnê hāʾĕlōhîm) of v.2 is the most debated phrase in Genesis. Three main traditions: (1) divine beings — the term consistently refers to heavenly beings in Job 1:6 and the pre-Christian Jewish tradition (1 Enoch 6–11; Jubilees 5) unanimously reads them as fallen angelic Watchers; (2) Sethite men — the righteous line of Seth taking Cainite women; (3) rulers/dynasts — elohim occasionally means rulers. The Nephilim (nĕpîlîm) in v.4 — 'fallen ones' or 'those who fall upon' — are associated with the Anakim in Numbers 13:33; in ANE tradition semi-divine warrior-heroes were a common cultural category. The 120 years of v.3 may be either a new human lifespan limit or the time remaining before the flood. Divine grief (wayyinnāḥem, v.6) uses the same root that names Noah (Genesis 5:29: 'he will comfort/nāḥam us') — the naming of Noah anticipates the narrative turn of v.8.
1.literary_structure: The section moves from demographic expansion (v.1) through the boundary-transgression (vv.2–4) to divine perception of universal evil (vv.5–7) and the structural pivot (v.8). Verses 5–7 use three intensifying terms: wickedness is 'great' (rabbāh), every intent 'only evil' (raq rāʿ), 'continually' (kol-hayyôm). This threefold emphasis establishes the totality of the judgment's necessity. The pivot of v.8 — 'But Noah found favor (ḥēn)' — is the structural counterpart to the 'But God' pivots of the NT: against the backdrop of universal condemnation, the grace-exception appears. The ḥēn/Noaḥ wordplay (favor/Noah) connects his very name to the grace he embodies.
1.theological_application: Gill on v.5: 'the wickedness of man was great in the earth — it spread throughout the earth, wherever it was inhabited by men, both among the posterity of Cain and Seth, and who indeed now were mixed together … they were corrupt in the worship of God, worshipping the creature more, or besides the Creator.' On divine grief (v.6): 'not that repentance, properly speaking, can fall upon God, for he never changes his mind or alters his purposes, though he may change his outward dispensations; but his conduct towards men bears a resemblance to that of one repenting.' Matthew Henry on the universality of v.5: 'Observe the comprehensive description of human nature — all the imagination of the thoughts of the heart was only evil continually. We see corruption here in its source: it begins with the heart; it appears in the thoughts; thoughts are the first-born of the soul, and these were all vile.' The grace of v.8 is the theological anchor: against universal condemnation, God's preserving grace singles out one man — not on the basis of his merit but as the foundation of the covenant that will follow.
1.christology: The divine grief of v.6 — 'it grieved him to his heart' (wayyitʿaṣṣēḇ ʾel-libbô) — is the first strong statement of divine pathos in response to human sin in Genesis. This is not a prelude to mere destruction; the grief produces both the judgment and the preservation of Noah, foreshadowing the pattern of the cross: the Son who 'bore our griefs' (Isaiah 53:4) is the one whose suffering produces the salvation of the remnant. Noah 'found favor (ḥēn)' — the OT's earliest use of the grace-word in a context of universal condemnation — and the NT makes explicit what Genesis implies: the grace that preserves the remnant through judgment is the grace that comes in Christ (John 1:17: 'grace and truth came through Jesus Christ').

9.synthesis: 'These are the generations of Noah. Noah was a righteous man, blameless in his generation. Noah walked with God' (v.9). The three-term description of Noah — righteous (ṣaddîq), blameless (tāmîm), walking with God — is the most comprehensive character commendation in Genesis to this point. God's communication to Noah is direct and devastating: the earth is filled with violence; the end of all flesh is decreed. Then the detailed ark-commission: dimensions, decks, door, window, provisions — and the first use of 'covenant' (bĕrît) in the Bible: 'But I will establish my covenant with you' (v.18). The section closes with the obedience summary: 'Noah did this; he did all that God commanded him' (v.22).
9.historical_context: The ark (tēḇāh) is one of only two uses of this word in the OT — the other being Moses's basket-ark in the Nile (Exodus 2:3), also coated with pitch. The pitch-coating (kōper) shares its root with kippēr (to cover/atone) — the atonement-covering language of the Levitical system is embedded in the ark's construction. The ark's dimensions (300 × 50 × 30 cubits) yield a 6:1 length-to-breadth ratio — the standard barge ratio for stability — and are remarkably practical compared to the cube-shaped Gilgamesh ark. The first use of 'covenant' in v.18 is significant: it appears within the decree of judgment, establishing YHWH's unilateral commitment to Noah before the flood begins, before the sacrifice is offered — grace precedes merit. Noah's obedience formula in v.22 (kĕkōl ʾăšer ṣiwwāh ʾōtô ʾĕlōhîm kēn ʿāśāh) recurs for Moses at the tabernacle (Exodus 39:43) and Joshua at the conquest.
9.literary_structure: The section opens with the triple character-affirmation (v.9), then presents God's speech (vv.11–21) in two parts: the judgment decree (vv.11–13) and the ark-commission (vv.14–21). The commission is meticulous — dimensions, materials, decks, provisions, animal categories — establishing that the preservation is as deliberate and ordered as the judgment is comprehensive. The covenant-announcement (v.18) stands at the center of the ark-commission, structurally indicating that the purpose of the preservation is not merely survival but covenantal relationship. The obedience-summary (v.22) closes the unit with narrative economy: every specific command of vv.14–21 is covered by 'all that God commanded him.'
9.theological_application: Gill on Noah's threefold character (v.9): 'A just and righteous man in his own person, having the righteousness of Christ imputed to him; and holy and blameless in his conversation, walking according to the laws and commands of God … blameless in his generation, that is, with respect to the men of his age, who were grossly impure and wicked; he, like the lily among thorns, was pure among them: and he walked with God, as Enoch did before him.' On the covenant of v.18: 'this covenant respects that later mentioned … that the world should be no more destroyed by water.' Matthew Henry on Noah's obedience: 'Observe how ready Noah was in obedience to God … he took care to do all that God commanded him, not one thing omitted. This is a great example, a standard and pattern of obedience: do all that God commands, and do it exactly as commanded.'
9.christology: The ark is the most sustained typological image of salvation in the pre-Abrahamic narrative. Peter makes it explicit: 'God waited patiently while the ark was being constructed … in this ark a few people, only eight souls, were saved through water, and this water symbolizes baptism that now saves you also — not the removal of dirt from the body but the pledge of a clear conscience toward God. It saves you by the resurrection of Jesus Christ' (1 Peter 3:20–21). The ark-as-Christ is the patristic consensus: entry into the ark = union with Christ; the one door = the one way (John 10:9); the pitch-covering (kōper) = atonement. The first 'covenant' in the Bible is established with a righteous man in the context of universal judgment — precisely the pattern of the new covenant, established with the Righteous One in the context of bearing the world's judgment.
